flashcards.definition: Interdisciplinary study of how humans interact with the natural environment.
Meeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs (Brundtland definition).
Caused or influenced by human activity (e.g., anthropogenic climate change).
A community of organisms interacting with their physical environment as a functional unit.
Systematic process: observation → hypothesis → experiment → analysis → conclusion.
Study of interactions between organisms and their environment; a foundational discipline of environmental science.
Study of harmful effects of chemicals on organisms; key for pollution assessment.
Applies economic principles to environmental issues (cost-benefit analysis, externalities, valuation).
Philosophical examination of moral relationships between humans and the natural world.
Geographic Information Systems; spatial analysis tools for mapping and analyzing environmental data.
Organisms that make their own food from inorganic matter (photosynthesis or chemosynthesis).
Organisms that obtain energy by eating other organisms.
Organisms (bacteria, fungi) that break down dead organic matter, recycling nutrients back to the ecosystem.
A feeding position in a food chain (producer = level 1, primary consumer = level 2, etc.).
Approximately 10% of energy is transferred from one trophic level to the next; ~90% lost as metabolic heat.
Movement of carbon through atmosphere, biosphere, oceans, and lithosphere; photosynthesis and respiration are key processes.
Conversion of atmospheric N₂ into usable forms (NH₃) by bacteria or the Haber-Bosch process.
Conversion of nitrates back to N₂ gas by bacteria; returns nitrogen to the atmosphere.
Continuous movement of water: evaporation, condensation, precipitation, runoff, infiltration, transpiration.
Industrial process converting N₂ + H₂ into ammonia (NH₃) for fertilizer; doubled global food production.
Environmental, economic, and social dimensions — all must be balanced for true sustainability.
Measure of how much biologically productive land and water is needed to support a population's consumption.
Impact = Population × Affluence × Technology; framework for understanding human environmental impact.
United Nations Sustainable Development Goals; 17 goals for 2030 addressing poverty, health, environment, and more.
The world's stock of natural resources (air, water, soil, biodiversity) that provide ecosystem services.
1930s ecological disaster caused by drought and overplowing; massive topsoil erosion on the Great Plains.
Once the 4th-largest lake; shrank ~90% due to Soviet irrigation diversions; ecological and health disaster.
Seasonal thinning of stratospheric ozone over Antarctica caused by CFCs; allows harmful UV radiation.
1987 international agreement phasing out ozone-depleting substances (CFCs); widely considered a success.
Chlorofluorocarbons; industrial chemicals that destroy stratospheric ozone; banned under the Montreal Protocol.
System that exchanges both matter and energy with its surroundings (e.g., an ecosystem).
System that exchanges energy but not matter with its surroundings (Earth, approximately).
Amplifying loop: output reinforces the change (e.g., ice-albedo feedback accelerating warming).
Stabilizing loop: output counteracts the change (e.g., predator-prey regulation).
Properties of a system that arise from interactions of its parts and don't exist in individual components.
Gross Primary Productivity; total amount of energy fixed by photosynthesis in an ecosystem.
Net Primary Productivity; GPP minus plant respiration (R); energy available to consumers. NPP = GPP - R.
Energy is conserved; it can be transformed but never created or destroyed.
Every energy conversion increases entropy; energy is lost as heat (explains the 10% rule).
One-way movement of energy through trophic levels; unlike matter, energy is not recycled in ecosystems.
Linear pathway showing energy/nutrient transfer from one organism to the next.
Complex network of interconnected food chains in an ecosystem.
Species whose removal causes disproportionately large changes in its ecosystem (e.g., sea otters, wolves).
Ecological process where changes at one trophic level cause reciprocal changes through the food web.
Energy pathway starting with dead organic matter processed by decomposers and detritivores.
J-curve; population grows without limit: dN/dt = rN; occurs with unlimited resources.
S-curve; growth slows as population approaches carrying capacity: dN/dt = rN(1 − N/K).
Maximum population size an environment can sustain indefinitely given available resources.
Species producing many offspring with little parental care; rapid growth; short lifespan (insects, bacteria).
Species producing few offspring with extensive parental care; slow growth; long lifespan (elephants, whales).
Two species competing for the same limited resource cannot coexist indefinitely; one will be eliminated.
Species reduce competition by using different parts of a resource (e.g., different tree heights for feeding).
Symbiotic relationship benefiting both species (+/+); e.g., bees and flowers.
Symbiotic relationship where one benefits at the other's expense (+/−); e.g., ticks on deer.
Reciprocal evolutionary change between interacting species; e.g., predator-prey arms race.
Ecological succession starting on bare substrate with no soil; very slow (centuries).
Succession after disturbance that leaves soil intact; faster than primary (decades).
First organisms to colonize bare substrate (lichens, mosses in primary; weeds/grasses in secondary).
The relatively stable ecosystem that develops at the end of ecological succession.
An event that disrupts an ecosystem (fire, flood, storm, volcanic eruption, human activity).
Variety of life at genetic, species, and ecosystem levels.
Threats to biodiversity: Habitat destruction, Invasive species, Population growth, Pollution, Climate change, Overexploitation.
Species in danger of extinction throughout all or a significant portion of its range.
Breaking continuous habitat into smaller, isolated patches; reduces biodiversity and gene flow.
Current ongoing extinction event driven by human activities; loss rate 100–1,000× the natural background rate.
Wolf reintroduction reduced elk overgrazing, allowing vegetation recovery and stream restoration.
Corals expel symbiotic algae (zooxanthellae) under heat stress, turning white; can lead to death if prolonged.
Theory that ~20-25% deforestation could trigger irreversible conversion of Amazon forest to savanna.
Symbiotic algae living in coral tissue; provide up to 90% of the coral's energy through photosynthesis.
Region with exceptionally high species richness and endemism that is significantly threatened by human activities.
Doubling time = 70 ÷ growth rate (in percent); quick estimation of how fast a population doubles.
Per capita rate of population change; r = birth rate − death rate (for closed populations).
Maximum population growth rate occurs at N = K/2 (inflection point of the S-curve).
ΔN = (births + immigration) − (deaths + emigration); comprehensive population accounting.
Time for a population to double in size; shorter doubling time = faster growth.
Average number of children born per woman during her lifetime.
TFR of ~2.1 needed to maintain population size without migration.
Human population grew slowly for millennia, then surged exponentially after ~1800 due to the Industrial Revolution.
Tendency for population to continue growing even after fertility drops to replacement, because large young cohorts enter reproductive age.
When birth rate equals death rate and population size stabilizes.
Four-stage model showing how populations shift from high birth/death rates to low rates with economic development.
High birth rate + high death rate → little or no population growth.
Death rate drops while birth rate stays high → rapid population growth.
Birth rate declining; death rate low → growth rate slowing.
Low birth + low death rates → slow or zero population growth.
Graph showing population distribution by age and sex; also called population pyramid.
Wide base (many young); indicates rapid population growth.
Column shape (equal ages); indicates stable population.
Narrow base (fewer young); indicates declining population.
Ratio of dependents (age 0–14 and 65+) to working-age population (15–64).
Total area of productive land and water required to support a person's resource consumption and waste.
Annual date when humanity has consumed more resources than Earth can regenerate in a year.
Earth's biological ability to regenerate resources and absorb waste in a given year.
When resource consumption exceeds biocapacity; humanity currently uses ~1.7 Earths of resources.
Average footprint per person; varies enormously by country (US ~8 gha vs. India ~1.2 gha).
Urban areas being warmer than surrounding rural areas due to concrete, asphalt, reduced vegetation, and waste heat.
Low-density, car-dependent suburban expansion that converts farmland and natural habitat.
Urban planning strategy: compact, walkable, mixed-use, transit-oriented development to reduce sprawl.
Roads, rooftops, and pavement that prevent water infiltration → increased runoff and flooding.
Movement of people from rural to urban areas; >56% of the world's population now lives in cities.
1980–2015 policy limiting most families to one child; reduced TFR but caused demographic imbalance.
Indian state that achieved low fertility (~1.6 TFR) through female education and healthcare, without coercion.
Government policies encouraging higher birth rates (e.g., France, Sweden, South Korea offering incentives).
Government policies discouraging births/reducing population growth (e.g., China's one-child policy).
Education, economic opportunities, and reproductive rights for women — the most effective way to reduce fertility.
Number of live births per 1,000 people per year.
Number of deaths per 1,000 people per year.
(CBR − CDR) / 10 → expressed as a percentage. Does not include migration.
(Immigration − Emigration) per 1,000 people per year.
Number of infant deaths (<1 year old) per 1,000 live births; key indicator of development.
Resource replenished naturally on a human timescale (solar, wind, water, biomass).
Resource formed over geological time; finite supply (fossil fuels, minerals, metals).
Maximum amount of a renewable resource that can be harvested without depleting stock.
Using a resource faster than it can be replaced, leading to decline or exhaustion.
Resource that is virtually inexhaustible on any human timescale (e.g., solar energy, tidal energy).
Underground layer of permeable rock or sediment that holds and transmits groundwater.
Massive US aquifer underlying the Great Plains; being depleted by irrigation faster than it recharges.
Pollution from a single identifiable source (e.g., factory pipe, sewage outfall).
Pollution from diffuse sources (e.g., agricultural runoff, urban stormwater).
Upper surface of the zone of saturation in an aquifer; drops when pumping exceeds recharge.
Layers of soil: O (organic), A (topsoil), B (subsoil), C (weathered parent material), R (bedrock).
Uppermost mineral layer; richest in organic matter and nutrients; essential for agriculture.
Removal of topsoil by wind and water; accelerated by deforestation, overgrazing, and poor farming practices.
Buildup of salts in soil from evaporation of irrigation water; reduces soil fertility.
1960s–70s agricultural transformation using high-yield varieties, fertilizers, pesticides, and irrigation.
Permanent clearing of forests for other land uses (agriculture, ranching, urban development).
Forests absorb more CO₂ than they release; loss converts them to carbon sources.
Mature forest that has not been significantly disturbed; highest biodiversity and carbon storage.
Replanting trees on previously forested land.
Managing forests to meet current timber needs without compromising future forest health or biodiversity.
Largest harvest that can be taken from a fish stock indefinitely without depleting it.
Harvesting fish faster than they can reproduce, leading to population decline.
Unintended capture of non-target species (dolphins, sea turtles, sharks) in fishing operations.
Farming of fish, shellfish, or aquatic plants; provides >50% of fish consumed globally.
Destructive fishing method dragging heavy nets along the ocean floor, damaging seafloor habitats.
Soviet-era irrigation diversions caused one of the worst environmental disasters; Aral Sea lost ~90% volume.
World's largest hydroelectric dam on China's Yangtze River; enormous power but major social and environmental costs.
US Great Plains aquifer being drained for irrigation faster than recharge; threatens food production.
Minerals mined in conflict zones (coltan, tin, tungsten, gold) whose trade funds armed groups.
Paradox where countries rich in natural resources often have poor economic outcomes and governance.
Shared resources depleted when individuals act in self-interest; solutions include regulation, privatization, and community management.
Economic tool comparing costs and benefits of a project or policy; often fails to account for environmental externalities.
A cost or benefit not reflected in market prices (e.g., pollution costs borne by society, not the polluter).
When potential for harm exists but science is uncertain, take preventive action to avoid environmental damage.
Flexible resource management approach that adjusts strategies based on monitoring and new data.
Coal, oil, and natural gas; nonrenewable energy sources formed from ancient organic matter over millions of years.
Solid fossil fuel; dirtiest per unit energy; used mainly for electricity generation; produces more CO₂, SO₂, and particulates.
Cleanest-burning fossil fuel (~50% less CO₂ than coal); primarily methane (CH₄); growing share of energy.
Injecting high-pressure fluid into rock to fracture it and release oil/gas; concerns about water contamination and earthquakes.
Precipitation with pH < 5.6 caused by SO₂ and NOₓ from fossil fuel combustion reacting with atmospheric water.
Splitting heavy nuclei (U-235, Pu-239) to release energy; used in current nuclear power plants.
Spent nuclear fuel and contaminated materials; high-level waste remains hazardous for thousands of years.
Time for half of a radioactive substance to decay; Pu-239 has a half-life of ~24,000 years.
Uncontrolled reactor core overheating; e.g., Chernobyl (1986), Fukushima (2011).
Combining light nuclei (hydrogen) to release energy; powers the sun but not yet commercially viable on Earth.
Semiconductor device that converts sunlight directly into electricity.
Device converting kinetic energy of wind into electrical energy.
Electricity generated by flowing water through turbines in dams; largest renewable source globally.
Heat energy from Earth's interior used for electricity generation or direct heating.
Challenge of solar and wind: output varies with weather and time of day; requires storage or backup.
Getting more useful output from less energy input (e.g., LED vs. incandescent bulbs).
Reducing energy use through behavioral changes (e.g., turning off lights, lowering thermostat).
Combined Heat and Power — using waste heat from electricity production for heating; ~80% total efficiency.
US EPA/DOE program certifying energy-efficient appliances and buildings.
A unit of energy saved through efficiency; often cheaper than generating new energy.
Germany's energy transition policy: ambitious shift from fossil fuels and nuclear to renewable energy.
State-level US policy requiring utilities to source a minimum percentage of electricity from renewables.
Policy guaranteeing renewable energy producers a fixed price per kWh, incentivizing investment.
Tax on fossil fuel carbon content designed to internalize the cost of CO₂ emissions.
Largest US climate investment: ~$370 billion in clean energy tax credits and incentives.
Useful energy output ÷ total energy input × 100%; always < 100% due to second law of thermodynamics.
Unit of energy: 1 kW of power used for 1 hour; equals 3.6 × 10⁶ joules.
Rate of energy use: 1 watt = 1 joule per second; 1 kilowatt = 1,000 watts.
Actual energy output / maximum possible output × 100% (e.g., solar ~25%, nuclear ~90%, wind ~35%).
Ratio of energy delivered by a source to energy invested to produce it; higher is better.
Pollutant emitted directly into the atmosphere (CO, SO₂, NOₓ, PM, VOCs, lead).
Formed by chemical reactions of primary pollutants in the atmosphere (ozone, smog, acid deposition).
Six pollutants regulated by the Clean Air Act: CO, Pb (lead), NO₂, O₃ (ozone), PM, SO₂.
Brown haze formed when sunlight reacts with NOₓ and VOCs; worst in sunny cities with heavy traffic (LA).
Warm air layer traps cool air and pollutants near the ground, worsening air quality.
Excess nutrient enrichment (N, P) → algal blooms → decomposition depletes oxygen → dead zones.
Amount of O₂ needed by microorganisms to decompose organic matter; high BOD = polluted water.
Area of very low dissolved oxygen (hypoxia) where most aquatic life cannot survive.
Disease-causing organism (bacteria, viruses, parasites) in contaminated water.
Buildup of a persistent chemical in an organism's body over time (e.g., mercury in fish).
Abandoned or underused industrial/commercial site with real or suspected contamination.
US law (1980) establishing a program to clean up the nation's worst hazardous waste sites.
Using living organisms (bacteria, fungi, plants) to break down or absorb soil and water contaminants.
Using plants to absorb, accumulate, or break down contaminants in soil or water.
Decline in land productivity due to erosion, contamination, salinization, or desertification.
Everyday trash generated by households and businesses; ~292 million tons/year in the US.
Preferred order: Reduce → Reuse → Recycle/Compost → Energy Recovery → Landfill (least preferred).
Percentage of waste diverted from landfills through recycling and composting; US ~32%.
Incineration of waste to generate electricity; reduces landfill volume but produces ash and emissions.
Discarded electronics containing valuable metals and hazardous materials; fastest-growing waste stream.
Waste with properties that make it dangerous: ignitable, corrosive, reactive, or toxic.
Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (1976); regulates hazardous waste from cradle to grave in the US.
RCRA system tracking hazardous waste from generation through transport, treatment, storage, and disposal.
All recyclables placed in one bin; convenient but increases contamination (non-recyclable items mixed in).
Recycling material into a lower-quality product (e.g., plastic bottles → carpet fiber); common for plastics.
US cap-and-trade program for SO₂ under 1990 Clean Air Act Amendments; reduced SO₂ by ~90%.
1952 killer smog (coal smoke + fog) that killed ~12,000 people; led to UK Clean Air Act.
2014 lead contamination when Flint, MI switched water sources; a major environmental justice failure.
Market-based system: government caps total pollutant emissions; companies trade emission allowances.
The fair treatment of all people regardless of race, income, or origin in environmental policy and protection.
Lethal Dose 50: the dose of a substance that kills 50% of a test population; measure of acute toxicity.
Graph showing the relationship between exposure dose and biological response/health effect.
No Observed Adverse Effect Level: highest dose with no measurable harmful effect.
Ratio of pollutant concentration in an organism to its concentration in the surrounding environment.
Time for half of a pollutant to break down or be removed from the environment.
Natural process: greenhouse gases trap infrared radiation, warming Earth ~33°C above what it would be without an atmosphere.
Human-caused increase in greenhouse gas concentrations intensifying the natural greenhouse effect → global warming.
Pre-industrial: ~280 ppm; 2024: ~424 ppm — a 50%+ increase primarily from fossil fuel burning and deforestation.
Change in net energy balance at the top of the atmosphere; positive forcing warms, negative cools.
Measure comparing a greenhouse gas's heat-trapping ability to CO₂ over a set period (e.g., CH₄ GWP ≈ 80 over 20 years).
Cylinders of ice drilled from glaciers that contain trapped air bubbles recording past atmospheric composition.
Global sea levels have risen ~20 cm since 1900; caused by thermal expansion and ice sheet melting.
Water expands as it warms; accounts for ~50% of observed sea level rise.
Oceans absorb CO₂, forming carbonic acid; pH has dropped ~0.1 units (30% more acidic) since pre-industrial times.
Indirect evidence of past climate from tree rings, ice cores, coral, and sediment (before instrument records).
Species moving to higher latitudes or elevations in response to warming temperatures.
When timing of ecological events (flowering, migration) becomes out of sync between interdependent species.
Person displaced by climate-related environmental changes (sea level rise, drought, extreme weather).
Reliable access to sufficient, safe, nutritious food; threatened by changing precipitation, temperature, and extreme events.
Diseases transmitted by organisms like mosquitoes and ticks; expanding range as warm areas expand (malaria, dengue, Lyme).
Actions that reduce the sources or enhance the sinks of greenhouse gases (addressing the cause).
Actions that adjust to current or expected climate change impacts (addressing the effects).
Capturing and storing CO₂ from the atmosphere (forests, soils, carbon capture technology).
Making fossil fuels more expensive through taxes or cap-and-trade to reflect their climate costs.
The ability of a system (ecosystem, community) to withstand and recover from climate impacts.
1997 international treaty requiring developed nations to reduce greenhouse gas emissions ~5% below 1990 levels.
2015 global climate accord: nearly all nations committed to limiting warming to well below 2°C, preferably 1.5°C.
Nationally Determined Contributions: each country's self-set climate targets under the Paris Agreement.
United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change: the parent treaty for Kyoto and Paris agreements.
Principle that all nations share climate responsibility but developed nations bear greater obligation due to historical emissions.
European Union Emissions Trading System: world's largest cap-and-trade carbon market since 2005.
Comprehensive state-level climate policy including cap-and-trade, 100% clean electricity target, and EV mandates.
Largest global emitter (~30% of CO₂) but also the world's largest investor in renewable energy.
Tax on imports from countries with weaker climate policies to prevent 'carbon leakage' (EU CBAM).
Climate policies by states, cities, and regions — often more ambitious than national governments.
Computer simulation of Earth's climate system using physical equations for atmosphere, ocean, land, and ice.
Scenarios of future greenhouse gas concentrations used in climate modeling: 2.6 (low) to 8.5 (high).
Updated scenarios combining socioeconomic development with emission pathways for climate projections.
Temperature increase expected from doubling atmospheric CO₂; estimated 2.5–4.0°C.
Total remaining CO₂ emissions allowed to stay below a specific temperature target.
Philosophy of protecting wilderness in its natural state (John Muir, Sierra Club).
Philosophy of managing natural resources for sustainable human use (Gifford Pinchot, 'wise use').
Rachel Carson's 1962 book exposing DDT's ecological damage; catalyzed the modern environmental movement and led to DDT ban.
First celebrated April 22, 1970; 20 million participants; led directly to the creation of the EPA.
Movement addressing the disproportionate environmental burdens on minority and low-income communities.
National Environmental Policy Act (1970): requires Environmental Impact Statements for major federal projects.
Federal law (1970) setting National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS) for criteria pollutants.
Federal law (1972) regulating point-source water pollution through NPDES permits.
Federal law (1973) protecting listed threatened and endangered species and their critical habitat.
Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act (1980): cleans up contaminated sites using 'polluter pays' principle.
United Nations Environment Programme, established 1972 at the Stockholm Conference to coordinate global environmental action.
1987 treaty phasing out CFCs and other ozone-depleting substances; ozone layer now recovering.
Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species: regulates and monitors international wildlife trade.
1992 treaty for conservation and sustainable use of biodiversity; includes Kunming-Montreal '30 by 30' framework.
Goal from 2022 Kunming-Montreal Framework to protect 30% of Earth's land and ocean by 2030.
Fair treatment and meaningful involvement of all people regardless of race, income, or origin in environmental decisions.
Disproportionate siting of environmentally hazardous facilities in communities of color.
2014–present crisis: lead contamination from changed water source in Flint, MI, disproportionately affecting Black residents.
85-mile stretch along the Mississippi River in Louisiana with concentrated petrochemical plants and elevated cancer rates in predominantly Black communities.
1994 presidential order requiring federal agencies to identify and address disproportionate environmental and health effects on minority and low-income populations.
Clean Air Act Title IV cap-and-trade that reduced SO₂ emissions ~90% — one of the most successful environmental regulations.
National Priorities List of ~1,300+ contaminated sites; cleanup has been slow and expensive since 1980.
Listing northern spotted owl under ESA led to logging restrictions in Pacific Northwest — economy vs. conservation debate.
Policy approach of 'learn by doing' — implementing, monitoring, and adjusting management strategies based on results.
Measured by whether laws achieve their environmental goals, at what cost, and with what distributional effects.
Government sets specific standards or technology requirements that regulated entities must meet.
Use economic incentives (taxes, cap-and-trade, subsidies) to achieve environmental goals at lower cost.
Systematic comparison of the costs and benefits of a policy to determine if benefits justify costs.
Command-and-control provides certainty of outcome; market-based provides cost-efficiency — often used together.
Problem identification → policy description → effectiveness evaluation → trade-off analysis → improvement proposals.
Meeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet their needs (Brundtland definition).
Environmental, economic, and social sustainability — all three must be balanced for true sustainability.
17 Sustainable Development Goals (2015–2030) addressing poverty, inequality, environment, and prosperity.
Measure of human demand on Earth's ecosystems in terms of area needed to produce resources and absorb waste.
Economic model that eliminates waste by redesigning products for reuse, repair, remanufacturing, and recycling.
Approach minimizing pesticide use through biological controls, crop rotation, resistant varieties, and targeted chemical use as last resort.
Plants grown between cash crop seasons to prevent erosion, build soil health, and sequester carbon.
Planting without plowing to reduce soil erosion, maintain soil structure, and keep carbon in the soil.
Practice of integrating trees with crop or livestock systems for environmental and economic benefits.
Hypoxic areas in water bodies caused by excess nutrient runoff (fertilizers) leading to algal blooms and oxygen depletion.
Total cost of building and operating a power source divided by total energy output — renewables now cheapest in most places.
Large battery systems that store renewable energy for use when sun isn't shining or wind isn't blowing.
Using less energy to provide the same service — the cheapest, cleanest form of 'energy production.'
Replacing fossil fuel use with electricity (EVs, heat pumps) so renewable electricity can power everything.
Policy allowing solar panel owners to sell excess electricity back to the grid, credited on their bill.
Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design: green building rating system evaluating energy, water, materials, and indoor quality.
Urban planning approach promoting compact, walkable, mixed-use development that reduces sprawl and car dependence.
Cities are warmer than surrounding rural areas due to pavement, buildings, and reduced vegetation.
Vegetation-based systems (green roofs, rain gardens, bioswales, urban forests) managing stormwater and reducing heat.
Building design using sun orientation, thermal mass, and natural ventilation to heat/cool without mechanical systems.
Model green city: solar energy leader, car-free districts, 40%+ trips by bike/transit, passive solar buildings.
Payment for Ecosystem Services: farmers paid to protect forests for carbon, water, biodiversity and scenic services.
Gross National Happiness: national development philosophy prioritizing well-being and environmental conservation over GDP.
Car-free sustainable neighborhood in Freiburg: passive solar housing, district heating, community design.
Absorbing more CO₂ than emitting — Bhutan achieves this through extensive forest cover and low emissions.
I = P × A × T : Environmental Impact = Population × Affluence (consumption per capita) × Technology (impact per unit of consumption).
Total greenhouse gas emissions (in CO₂ equivalents) caused by an individual, event, product, or organization.
Human Development Index: composite measure of health (life expectancy), education, and standard of living.
Alternative to GDP that adds value of positive non-market activities and subtracts costs of environmental degradation and social problems.
The capacity of ecosystems to produce biological materials and absorb waste — compared to ecological footprint.
Energy moves through ecosystems in one direction: sun → producers → consumers → decomposers; 10% rule between trophic levels.
Carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, and water cycles — human activities have disrupted all of them.
Maximum population an environment can sustain; influenced by resources, space, disease, and predation.
Toxins concentrate in organisms (bioaccumulation) and increase at each trophic level (biomagnification).
Environmental, economic, and social sustainability must all be addressed for true sustainability.
80 MC questions (90 min, 60% of score) + 3 FRQs (70 min, 40% of score).
Design an Investigation, Analyze an Environmental Problem, and Propose a Solution.
AP response structure: make a claim → support with specific evidence → explain reasoning connecting evidence to claim.
APES requires calculations: per capita rates, percent change, population growth, energy conversions.
FRQs scored on point-based rubrics rewarding specific, correct answers
Largest US estuary; impaired by nutrient runoff from agriculture and development; multi-state cleanup effort.
Central Asian lake shrunk ~90% due to Soviet-era irrigation diversions — one of the worst environmental disasters.
Example of societal collapse from overexploitation of resources (deforestation → soil erosion → population decline).
World's largest hydroelectric dam (China): 22.5 GW capacity but displaced 1.3 million people and caused ecological damage.
Massive US aquifer underlying 8 Great Plains states; being depleted faster than recharge for irrigation (~30% depleted).
Public participation in scientific research: monitoring species, measuring air quality, reporting pollution.
Computer-based tools for mapping, analyzing, and visualizing environmental data spatially.
Designing solutions for environmental problems: water treatment, waste management, pollution control.
Using satellites and sensors to monitor environmental changes: deforestation, ice melt, water quality, and land use.
Growing fields: conservation biology, sustainability consulting, renewable energy, policy analysis, urban planning, environmental law.
Detailed document assessing the environmental effects of a proposed federal action, required under NEPA.
Description of the current state of the environment before the proposed project (the 'before' picture).
Actions to avoid, minimize, or compensate for negative environmental impacts of a project.
What happens if the proposed project is NOT undertaken — required comparison in all EIS documents.
Evaluation of combined effects of a project along with other past, present, and reasonably foreseeable future actions.
Understanding how environmental, economic, and social systems interact — the core skill of environmental science.
AP response framework: State a claim → Support with evidence → Explain reasoning connecting them.
Doubling time = 70 ÷ growth rate (%). Quick estimate for population or economic doubling.
Total ÷ population = per capita rate. Used for footprint, waste, water, energy comparisons.
Environmental science concepts are deeply connected: population → consumption → pollution → health → policy → sustainability.
